items.title: The Black Sox Scandal: Eight Players Accused of Throwing the 1919 World Series
items.hook: Gamblers met star pitchers, and America’s pastime faced its greatest corruption crisis.
items.angle: How the 1919 fix unraveled, why a jury acquitted the players, and how Judge Landis banned them anyway.

requiredSources.title: Chicago Historical Society on the Black Sox

items.title: Teapot Dome: When Cabinet Secretaries Sold Navy Oil Reserves
items.hook: Secret leases, private yachts, and the bribery scandal that defined 1920s Washington corruption.
items.angle: Interior Secretary Albert Fall, Wyoming oil fields, and the first cabinet member sent to prison for a crime in office.

requiredSources.title: Albert B. Fall and Teapot Dome

items.title: The Bonus Army: When Hoover Ordered Tanks Against WWI Veterans
items.hook: Thousands of unemployed veterans camped in Washington — until the Army drove them out with tear gas.
items.angle: The 1932 march, the Anacostia flats encampment, MacArthur’s controversial assault, and the political fallout.

items.title: The Tulsa Race Massacre: When a Prosperous Black District Was Destroyed
items.hook: Greenwood burned for days; hundreds died; and the story was buried for generations.
items.angle: The 1921 attack on Black Wall Street, aerial bombardment claims, mass graves, and the 2021 centennial reckoning.

requiredSources.title: 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre
requiredSources.publisher: National Museum of African American History and Culture

items.title: MOVE: When Philadelphia Police Dropped a Bomb on a City Block
items.hook: A helicopter-delivered explosive killed 11 people and burned 61 homes in West Philadelphia.
items.angle: The 1985 confrontation, the radical group MOVE, Mayor Goode, and why the city formally apologized decades later.

items.title: The Ludlow Massacre: When Colorado Militia Killed Striking Miners’ Families
items.hook: A tent colony burned; two women and eleven children died underground; and labor war erupted across the coalfields.
items.angle: The 1914 Colorado coal strike, Rockefeller ownership, National Guard machine guns, and federal intervention.

items.title: The Wilmington Coup of 1898: America’s Only Violent Overthrow of a City Government
items.hook: White supremacists expelled elected officials, burned a Black newspaper, and killed dozens in North Carolina.
items.angle: The only successful coup on U.S. soil, the fusionist coalition it destroyed, and the 2021 state truth commission.

items.title: Lake Peigneur: When a Drill Bit Punched Into a Salt Mine and Swallowed a Lake
items.hook: A oil rig punctured a mine under a Louisiana lake — reversing the Delcambre Canal and pulling barges under.
items.angle: The 1980 disaster, the whirlpool, the missing island, and how everyone miraculously escaped the rig.

items.title: The Titan II Missile Explosion: When a Wrench Dropped on Arkansas
items.hook: A maintenance mishap vented fuel, exploded a nuclear missile silo, and killed one airman in 1980.
items.angle: The Damascus, Arkansas accident, the 9-megaton warhead that did not detonate, and Cold War near-miss protocols.

items.title: America’s Minuteman Missiles: The Accidents That Almost Started Nuclear War
items.hook: False alarms, dropped tools, and fire in silos — how the U.S. came close to launching by mistake.
items.angle: Declassified near-misses in the ICBM fleet, Goldsboro parallels, and why humans stayed in the loop.

items.title: The Tuskegee Study: When the U.S. Government Lied to Black Men for 40 Years
items.hook: Researchers watched syphilis progress without treatment — long after penicillin existed.
items.angle: Macon County, Alabama; the 1932–1972 study; whistleblowers; and the Belmont Report that followed.

items.title: The Poison Squad: When Civil Servants Ate Borax to Prove Food Was Fake
items.hook: Harvey Wiley’s volunteers swallowed preservatives so Congress would pass the Pure Food and Drug Act.
items.angle: The 1902 dining room at the USDA, public stunt science, and the birth of FDA food regulation.

items.title: The Osage Murders: When Oil Wealth Made Native Headrights Worth Killing For
items.hook: Dozens of Osage citizens died under suspicious circumstances in 1920s Oklahoma — a conspiracy for oil money.
items.angle: The Reign of Terror, the FBI’s birth as a detective bureau, and the Killers of the Flower Moon case.

items.title: Wounded Knee 1973: The 71-Day Siege That Put Native Rights on National TV
items.hook: AIM activists seized a town; the FBI surrounded them; and two federal agents died in the standoff’s aftermath.
items.angle: The occupation, treaty violations cited by protesters, and the controversial trials that followed.

items.title: The Business Plot: Did Wall Street Try to Install a Fascist Dictator in 1934?
items.hook: A retired general told Congress that financiers asked him to lead 500,000 veterans against FDR.
items.angle: Smedley Butler’s testimony, the McCormack–Dickstein Committee, and why no one was prosecuted.

items.title: Operation Northwoods: The Pentagon Plan to Stage Attacks on Americans
items.hook: Declassified proposals included sinking refugee boats and faking hijackings to justify war with Cuba.
items.angle: The 1962 JCS memoranda, Kennedy’s rejection, and what the documents reveal about Cold War thinking.

items.hook: The U.S. brought V-2 engineers to Huntsville — sanitizing records and overlooking war crimes.
items.angle: Von Braun, Fort Bliss, Huntsville rocketry, and the moral compromise behind the moon program’s roots.

items.title: The Oregon Balloon Bomb: When WWII Reached the Continental U.S. and Killed Civilians
items.hook: Japan launched thousands of fire balloons; one killed a pastor’s wife and five children on a church picnic.
items.angle: Fu-Go weapons, censorship of U.S. press, and the only WWII combat deaths on the American mainland.

items.title: The Greenbrier Bunker: Congress’s Secret Cold War Hideaway Under a Resort
items.hook: For decades, a luxury hotel concealed an underground chamber meant for the entire U.S. legislature.
items.angle: Project Greek Island, fallout protection, exposure in 1992, and the tourism tours that followed.

items.angle: Manhattan Project production, Green Run, declassified documents, and the largest environmental cleanup in U.S. history.

items.title: Centralia: The Pennsylvania Town That Has Burned Underground Since 1962
items.hook: A coal seam fire still smolders beneath empty streets — most residents left decades ago.
items.angle: The dump fire that ignited the vein, failed containment, eminent domain, and the ghost town left behind.

items.title: The Peshtigo Fire: The Deadliest Wildfire in American History — Overshadowed by Chicago
items.hook: On the same night as the Great Chicago Fire, Wisconsin burned — killing an estimated 1,500 people.
items.angle: The fire tornado, logging towns erased, and why the Midwest disaster vanished from national memory.

items.title: The Cocoanut Grove Fire: 492 Dead in Boston’s Deadliest Nightclub Disaster
items.hook: Revolving doors jammed, decorations ignited in seconds, and fire codes changed nationwide.
items.angle: The 1942 Boston blaze, illegal occupancy, heroism and panic, and the medical advances in burn care that followed.

items.title: The Hartford Circus Fire: When the Big Top Became a Death Trap in Minutes
items.hook: A waterproofed tent burned in minutes; 167 died — many unidentified for decades.
items.angle: The 1944 Ringling show, the ‘Little Miss 1565’ mystery, and circus safety reforms.

items.title: The Sultana: America’s Worst Maritime Disaster — and Almost Nobody Remembers It
items.hook: An overcrowded Mississippi steamboat boiler exploded, killing 1,100+ — mostly freed Union POWs.
items.angle: The 1865 tragedy buried by Lincoln’s assassination news, bribery of inspectors, and legal impunity.

requiredSources.title: Sultana explosion
requiredSources.publisher: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

items.title: The SS Eastland: 844 Dead When a Pleasure Boat Capsized in Chicago Harbor
items.hook: Western Electric workers boarded for a picnic — the ship rolled over in 20 feet of water.
items.angle: The 1915 Chicago disaster, design instability, trapped passengers, and reforms in load limits.

items.title: The Bath School Disaster: When a Board Treasurer Bombed Michigan Children
items.hook: Andrew Kehoe killed 38 elementary students and six adults — America’s deadliest school attack for decades.
items.angle: The 1927 bombing, financial motives, the dynamite-rigged building, and Kehoe’s final explosion.

items.title: The Triangle Shirtwaist Fire: 146 Garment Workers Locked Inside a Burning Factory
items.hook: Young women jumped from windows; the tragedy sparked labor law and fire exit reforms.
items.angle: The 1911 New York blaze, locked doors, the trial that acquitted owners, and the unions’ response.
imageSearchTerms: Triangle Shirtwaist fire 1911 New York factory

items.title: Castle Bravo: When America’s Biggest H-Bomb Test Poisoned Pacific Islanders and Japanese Fishermen
items.hook: The 1954 Bikini test was twice as powerful as predicted — radioactive ash rained on Rongelap and the Lucky Dragon.
items.angle: U.S. Pacific testing, the 15-megaton miscalculation, downwind compensation fights, and nuclear diplomacy fallout.

items.title: The Haymarket Affair: A Bomb, a Riot, and the Labor Martyrs Who Shaped May Day
items.hook: Chicago’s 1886 rally for the eight-hour day ended in police deaths, show trials, and international labor memory.
items.angle: The bomb thrower never identified, anarchist defendants, pardons, and why May 1 became a global workers’ day.

items.title: The Whiskey Rebellion: When George Washington Led an Army Against Tax Protesters
items.hook: Frontier distillers resisted Hamilton’s tax — and the President marched 13,000 troops into Pennsylvania.
items.angle: The 1794 insurrection, federal authority tested, and how the crisis ended without major bloodshed.
